common_name: Hamlin Orange (Early Cold-Hardy Sweet Orange, Standard Florida Juice Orange)

planting.note: Plant grafted nursery trees in early spring after cold risk has passed; Hamlin is one of the hardier sweet orange varieties, tolerating brief temperatures to 26–27°F when the tree is established and cold-hardened — making it one of the most cold-tolerant choices for the northern margins of zone 9. In zone 9 (Louisiana, Mississippi Gulf Coast, eastern Texas), plant in a sheltered, south-facing location for maximum cold protection. Hamlin oranges ripen October through December — earlier than most other sweet oranges — making them valuable as the first tree-ripe citrus of the season. Fruit should be left on the tree until color has fully developed; unlike many citrus, Hamlin does not improve significantly in storage and is best used for juice immediately after harvest.

directions: Plant grafted tree in full sun, well-drained soil; protect from frost in zone 9 when young. Fruit in October–December. Hamlin Orange is the most commercially important sweet orange in Florida — the variety that produces the majority of Florida's fresh-squeezed and not-from-concentrate orange juice, and the dominant early-season orange in Florida commercial groves. The variety was discovered as a chance seedling in Glenwood, Florida in 1879 by A.G. Hamlin, who recognized its value and began propagating it; the combination of very early ripening (October–December harvest, months ahead of navel oranges), excellent cold hardiness for a sweet orange, prolific bearing, and high-quality juice made Hamlin rapidly popular among Florida citrus growers. The fruit is medium-sized, round, smooth-skinned, thin-skinned, with pale orange-yellow to bright orange exterior. The flesh is tender, very juicy, with excellent sweet-acid balance and nearly seedless character (typically 1–3 seeds per fruit) — qualities that make Hamlin ideal for juicing, where seed count affects yield and flavor extraction. Hamlin's cold hardiness is one of its most significant practical attributes for zone 9 gardeners: it is more reliably cold-hardy than navel orange, Valencia, or most other sweet orange varieties, making it the recommended choice for zone 9a plantings where severe freezes are possible. Established trees in good health can survive temperatures to 26°F briefly without significant damage, though young trees and flowers are more sensitive.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hamlin orange variety description and cold hardiness data
Florida Department of Agriculture — Commercial Orange Variety Production Statistics
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Citrus Production in the Gulf South

common_name: Ruby Red Grapefruit (Original Texas Pink Grapefruit, First US Plant Patent Recipient)

planting.note: Plant grafted nursery trees in spring; Ruby Red grapefruit is most successfully grown in the Rio Grande Valley of south Texas (zone 9b-10) and the Gulf Coast where the combination of warm summers and cooler winters produces the best red pigmentation development — the pink-red color intensifies with cooler temperatures and the fruit may remain pale in very mild winters. In zone 9 (Louisiana, Mississippi), grapefruit trees are near the northern cold limit and require frost protection when young and during severe cold events; mature trees can tolerate brief temperatures to 26–28°F. The fruit matures late (November–June), with peak quality typically in January–April. Ruby Red grapefruit should hang on the tree until fully mature — the bright pink flesh and sweet-tart flavor develop fully only at ripening and do not improve if picked early.

directions: Plant grafted tree in full sun, deep well-drained soil; mulch for moisture retention. Protect from frost in zone 9. Ruby Red is the original pink/red-fleshed grapefruit variety and one of the most historically significant citrus varieties in American horticulture: it holds the distinction of being the first variety to receive a US plant patent, issued February 4, 1930 (US Plant Patent 1), when Bonnie Nance's discovery of a pink bud sport on a Thompson grapefruit tree in Weslaco, Texas in 1929 was submitted by Stark Brothers Nursery. The story of Ruby Red begins in the Rio Grande Valley of Texas — one of the finest grapefruit-producing regions in the world — where the combination of warm days, cool nights, and alkaline soil produces grapefruit of exceptional sweetness and flavor. Ruby Red's pink flesh coloration comes from lycopene (the same pigment in tomatoes and watermelons) — a pigment that accumulates in the fruit's flesh during the cool nights of the growing season. The variety has less red pigmentation than its descendants (Rio Red and Star Ruby, which were developed specifically for deeper coloration), but it was the original breakthrough that made pink grapefruit a distinct commercial category. The fruit is large (3–5 inches), mildly fragrant, with blushed pink skin and sweet, mildly acidic, aromatic flesh that made pink grapefruit the preferred fresh-eating grapefruit variety in American markets through the mid-20th century.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Ruby Red grapefruit variety history and Texas citrus production
USDA Patent Office — US Plant Patent 1, February 4, 1930 (Ruby Red grapefruit)
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Grapefruit Variety Production and Cold Hardiness

common_name: Russet Burbank Potato (Classic Idaho Baking Potato, Most Widely Grown US Potato)

planting.note: Plant seed potato pieces (each with 2–3 eyes) in cool soil; Russet Burbank is a late-season variety requiring 80–100 days and a long cool season to develop its characteristic large, well-formed tubers. In zone 9, plant January–February for a spring harvest (April–June), before summer heat forces the plant into dormancy. Russet Burbank performs better in cool climates (zones 3–7) than in zone 9, where the brief cool window and tendency for early spring heat can limit tuber size and number; varieties such as Red Norland or Kennebec may perform more consistently in zone 9. However, the Russet Burbank can produce good crops in zone 9 with January planting in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil. Harvest when vine tops die back and skins have set. Cure in a cool, dark location for 10–14 days before storage.

directions: Plant certified seed potato pieces in trenches 4 inches deep; hill soil around plants as they grow. Harvest when tops die back. Russet Burbank is the most economically important potato variety in the United States — the potato that defined American french fry culture and continues to be the dominant variety grown commercially in Idaho, Washington, and Oregon. The variety's history traces to Luther Burbank, the famous plant breeder of Lunenburg, Massachusetts, who developed the 'Burbank' potato in 1875 — a white-skinned, prolific variety. A natural bud sport of the Burbank with netted russet skin emerged in the late 19th century and was selected for its superior baking qualities: the russet skin variety produced the floury, high-starch, dry flesh that makes for the ideal baked potato — a fluffy interior with a crisp, well-cured skin that holds its shape. The high starch content of Russet Burbank also makes it the preferred variety for making french fries: when fried, the high starch content produces the crispy exterior and fluffy interior texture that defines the classic fast-food french fry. McDonald's adopted Russet Burbank (specifically the 'Russet Burbank' and later selected clones) as the basis for their standardized french fry program, and this relationship made the variety synonymous with American potato processing. The tubers are very large (often 8–16 oz), oblong, with rough russet-brown skin and bright white flesh — the visual standard for 'Idaho potato' that most Americans associate with baked potatoes.
sources: University of Idaho Extension — Russet Burbank variety history and commercial potato production
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Potato variety database and commercial statistics
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cool-Season Potato Production in the Gulf South

common_name: French Fingerling Potato (Pink-Skinned Fingerling with Rose-Marbled Flesh)

planting.note: Plant seed potato pieces in cool soil with 2–3 eyes per piece; French Fingerling is a mid-season fingerling type that produces elongated, finger-shaped tubers 2–4 inches long with distinctive pink skin and cream to pale yellow flesh marbled with pink veins. In zone 9, plant January–February for a March–May harvest before summer heat arrives. The compact vines (18–24 inches) produce tubers in clusters that remain relatively close to the surface and are easily harvested by digging carefully around the plant. French Fingerling is not a traditional French heirloom in the way Rattes or other older varieties are, but the name reflects the variety's French-inspired culinary aesthetic and elegant appearance. Like most fingerling varieties, French Fingerling is waxy-fleshed and holds its shape well through roasting, sautéing, and boiling.

directions: Plant seed potato pieces 3–4 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil; harvest when vines begin to die back. French Fingerling is valued primarily for its visual distinction and culinary versatility: the combination of rose-pink skin and cream-yellow flesh with distinctive pink marbling through the interior makes French Fingerling one of the most visually striking fingerling varieties when halved or sliced — the pink marbling pattern within the creamy flesh is unique and makes the roasted or halved potato distinctly attractive in presentations. The texture is waxy and firm — a characteristic of fingerling potato varieties that have lower starch content than baking types (Russet Burbank) and retain their shape well through cooking. This waxy texture makes fingerlings the preferred choice for roasting whole (they develop a crispy exterior while staying firm inside), for potato salads (they don't become mushy when dressed), and for sautéed preparations where pieces need to hold their shape. French Fingerling's flavor is described as nutty, sweet, and complex — more interesting than the relatively neutral flavor of standard Russet types, with a richness that farmers market customers and restaurant chefs find particularly valuable. The variety performs well for small-scale and home garden production, producing enough tubers per plant for household use without requiring the large-scale inputs (cool storage, extensive land) of commercial Russet production.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — French Fingerling potato variety notes
Wood Prairie Farm — French Fingerling fingerling potato certified seed potato information
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Fingerling potato variety comparison for Southern gardens

common_name: Gregg's Mistflower (Gregg's Blue Boneset, Texas Native Monarch Butterfly Perennial)

planting.note: Plant divisions or nursery transplants in spring; Gregg's Mistflower spreads by rhizomes and forms colonies — a useful characteristic for erosion control and naturalizing in dry areas, but worth understanding before planting in confined garden spaces. In zone 9, this Texas native thrives in full sun to partial shade with excellent drainage and low water once established. The fall bloom season (August–November) coincides with monarch butterfly migration through Texas and the Gulf Coast — Gregg's Mistflower is among the most important nectar plants for monarch butterflies, queen butterflies, and many other nectaring species during this critical migration period. Cut plants to the ground in winter or early spring; vigorous new growth emerges from the root system each spring. Once established, Gregg's Mistflower requires virtually no supplemental irrigation in zone 9.

directions: Plant in well-drained to average soil in full sun or partial shade; water until established, then minimal irrigation. Cut to the ground in late winter. Gregg's Mistflower (Conoclinium greggii, formerly Eupatorium greggii) is a native perennial wildflower of the Texas Hill Country, Edwards Plateau, and Trans-Pecos regions, with a natural range extending into New Mexico and northern Mexico. The plant produces clusters of small lavender-blue to blue-purple powder-puff flower heads in dense flat-topped clusters from August through November — a bloom time precisely coinciding with the southward migration of monarch butterflies through Texas and the Gulf Coast states to their Mexican overwintering grounds. This timing has made Gregg's Mistflower one of the most recommended plants for monarch waystation gardens and wildlife habitat plantings in Texas and the Gulf South, where it provides nectar at a critical period when many other flowering plants have finished their season. The genus name Conoclinium (formerly Eupatorium for most mistflowers) places it in the boneset/Joe-Pye weed group of the aster family — native American plants with a long history of use as medicinal herbs and butterfly garden plants. Gregg's Mistflower distinguishes itself from the more widely distributed Blue Mistflower (Conoclinium coelestinum, eastern US native) by its preference for drier, more alkaline conditions — making it better adapted to the limestone soils and lower rainfall of central Texas and the western Gulf Coast than its eastern relative. The rhizomatous spreading habit gradually colonizes an area and provides increasingly effective erosion control on slopes and rocky terrain.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Conoclinium greggii native range and pollinator documentation
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Perennials for Texas Wildlife Gardens
North American Butterfly Association — Gregg's Mistflower as monarch butterfly nectar plant
